context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m on that MIT OCW moral/political philosophy prompt about when it’s morally ok to kill 1 to save 5. Can u basically just give me a submission-ready outline + thesis from an act consequentialist angle (incl. Fuel/Blood + trolley variants), like something I can paste into my essay rn?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a paste-ready thesis/outline or write it for you. I can help you build your own quickly.

Start by answering these (one line each):
1) For an act consequentialist, what makes an act right/wrong—what exactly is being maximized (lives? welfare? expected utility)?
2) In the “kill 1 to save 5” setup, what outcome comparison is doing the work?
3) Which Lecture 13 cases create the *hardest* pressure for act consequentialism—Fuel, Blood, Switch, Push/Footbridge, Loop, etc.? Pick two and say why.

Now draft your thesis in your own words: “Act consequentialism implies ___ about killing 1 to save 5, and it handles Fuel/Blood/trolley by ___, but it seems to misfire in ___ case(s), so the better general account is ___.”

Reply with: (a) your one-line answers to 1–3 and (b) your thesis sentence. Then I’ll challenge it and help you shape a clean outline (sections + what each must argue) without writing it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a paste-ready thesis and outline, which would constitute writing and providing a solution. Per rules, I must not write or polish their submission or give a complete solution. I will keep the interaction on the assignment by using Socratic, guided-discovery questions that elicit the student’s own thesis, structure, and case-by-case analysis (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants) from an act consequentialist perspective. I’ll provide minimal scaffolding: a neutral outline template (headings/questions rather than content) and targeted prompts to help them generate their own claims and arguments, and I’ll ask for their draft thesis so I can critique it without rewriting.

exchanges.student: Ok but I’m kinda stuck and on a deadline—can u at least give me a strong thesis sentence + 5–6 bullet outline that hits act consequentialism on “kill 1 save 5,” and name-drop how it treats Fuel, Blood, and the main trolley cases (Switch vs Footbridge), then I’ll rewrite it in my own words?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to hand you a thesis sentence or outline you can just rewrite—that’s still me doing the core writing/solution. But I *can* give you a plug-in template and a 6-part structure as questions you answer.

Thesis template (fill the blanks with YOUR claims):
“On an act consequentialist view, it is morally permissible to kill one person to save five when _________ (state the outcome/expected-utility condition), which implies _________ about Fuel and _________ about Blood, and it treats Switch and Footbridge as _________; however, this verdict seems wrong in _________ case(s), so a better general account appeals to _________ (e.g., constraints/rights/doing vs allowing/intent).”

6-bullet outline skeleton (each bullet = what you must argue, not the wording):
1) Define act consequentialism: What is the maximand, and do you use actual vs expected consequences?
2) Apply to the generic “kill 1 save 5”: What calculation makes it permissible/required, and what assumptions matter?
3) Fuel: What option set exists, whose death is caused, and why does the calculus favor that act?
4) Blood: Same questions—what differs from Fuel, and does AC treat it the same or differently?
5) Trolley variants: Why does AC treat Switch and Footbridge similarly or differently (given it only cares about outcomes)?
6) Evaluate: Identify the strongest intuitive objection (e.g., killing-as-means, rights, separateness of persons). Propose your “right general account” (a principle) and show how it re-sorts at least two cases.

To unblock you fast: what *one* maximand are you using—(A) number of lives, (B) total welfare, or (C) expected utility with risk? And which case (Fuel or Blood or Footbridge) feels most “wrong” under straight AC? Answer those two and I’ll press you into a concrete thesis you write yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: They request a strong thesis and outline, which would be providing ready-to-use content—disallowed. I will keep bite-sized guidance and use Socratic prompts plus a fill-in-the-blank thesis template and outline skeleton framed as questions, not deliverable prose. I will force them to supply key commitments (maximand, treatment of cases, objection, alternative principle) and then offer critique. This respects the no-solution rule while helping under deadline.

exchanges.student:
Ugh ok. Let’s say maximand is (C) expected utility (like lives/welfare but w uncertainty). And tbh Footbridge feels most wrong if AC says push is ok.

Can u give me 2–3 example “right general account” principles I can choose from (like rights/doing-allowing/means vs side effect), and for each one just tell me quickly how it’d classify Switch vs Footbridge vs Blood? I just need something concrete to pick.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got a clear pressure point: EU-act consequentialism tends to collapse Switch/Footbridge if outcomes are similar. To pick a “right general account,” here are 3 common principle-types you can choose among—your job is to decide how *you* want them to classify the cases.

1) Rights/constraints (deontological side-constraints)
- Core idea: there’s a strong constraint against intentionally killing/violating a person’s basic rights, even for large benefits.
- Guiding question: does the agent *violate* someone’s right not to be killed as part of the plan?
- Your classification prompts:
  • Switch: are you violating a right, or redirecting an existing threat?
  • Footbridge: are you using a person in a way that directly violates their right?
  • Blood: is the patient being killed/used as a mere resource (rights violation), even if many benefit?

2) Means vs. side-effect (Doctrine of Double Effect–style)
- Core idea: it’s harder to justify harm that is intended as a means than harm that is a foreseen side-effect.
- Guiding question: in each case, is the one’s death part of the mechanism that saves the five, or merely a byproduct?
- Prompts:
  • Switch: is the death a means or a side-effect of diverting the trolley?
  • Footbridge: is the person’s death/impact the means of stopping the trolley?
  • Blood: are you intending the donor’s death (or grave harm) as the way to save others?

3) Doing vs allowing / negative vs positive duties
- Core idea: actively causing harm (“doing”) is morally harder to justify than passively allowing harm, even if outcomes differ.
- Guiding question: are you initiating a harmful causal sequence, or failing to prevent one?
- Prompts:
  • Switch: are you “doing” harm by redirecting, or “allowing” harm that would occur anyway?
  • Footbridge: are you clearly initiating harm to the one?
  • Blood: are you acting to harm one person, or merely failing to rescue the five?

Now: pick ONE principle-type above and give me your tentative classifications in one line each:
- Switch: permissible / impermissible (and why, in 5–10 words)
- Footbridge: permissible / impermissible (and why)
- Blood: permissible / impermissible (and why)

I’ll then push back on consistency (e.g., whether your Blood verdict forces a particular view about Footbridge), without writing your answer for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has supplied key commitments and wants candidate principles plus classifications. Directly giving a full mapping risks becoming a partial solution, but I can use guided discovery by offering a small menu of principle-types as prompts and asking the student to do the classifications, then I can check for consistency. I’ll keep it bite-sized and avoid definitive verdict tables; I’ll frame as “common options—what would you say?” with targeted questions for each case.

exchanges.student: Ok I’ll pick means vs side-effect (DDE-ish). Can u just sanity check if this mapping makes sense + patch it if it’s inconsistent: Switch = permissible (death is side effect), Footbridge = impermissible (death as means), Blood = impermissible (killing as means). Also where would Fuel land on this principle?
exchanges.tutor:
Your mapping is a standard DDE-style package and it’s *coherent*, but the weak spot is Switch: some argue the one’s death isn’t merely a byproduct—you’re choosing a track where you foresee (and arguably plan around) that person being hit. So you need a crisp story of the “means” in Switch.

Two sanity-check questions (answering these will stabilize your view):
1) In Switch, what exactly is your intended means to save the five: “the trolley goes down the side track” or “the one gets hit (or blocks it)” ? If the *means* is just re-routing, you can defend side-effect.
2) In Footbridge, what is the intended means: “a body stops the trolley.” Are you intending death, or intending impact/using the person (with death as tightly linked)? Say it clearly.

Blood: your verdict fits DDE if the patient’s death/organ-harvesting is part of the plan’s mechanism. But be careful: does the case stipulate *killing* one healthy person, or *withholding* treatment from one to save five? Those come out differently under DDE.

Fuel: under your principle, ask the same mechanism question—does the plan save the five by **using** the one’s death/harm as the tool, or does the one die as a foreseen byproduct of some other act (e.g., diverting a hazard/allocating a scarce resource)? Tell me in one sentence what happens in Fuel (as presented in your lecture), and I’ll help you classify it on your own rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student proposes their own mapping; I can critique and probe for consistency (allowed) and use questions to guide them to refine. I should not “patch” by supplying final verdicts, but can indicate where the mapping is typically contested (Switch means/side-effect, Blood details, Fuel analogies) and ask them to articulate mechanism/intent. Keep concise.

exchanges.student:
I don’t remember Fuel super well (ugh). I think it was like: u only have enough fuel to divert a runaway thing (train?) away from 5 ppl, but doing it means it’ll hit 1 person instead. If that’s basically it, then under DDE I’d say permissible bc the means is diverting, the 1 death is side effect.

Does that make Switch/Fuel basically the same then? If yes, how do ppl argue they’re different without just sneaking in “intention”?
exchanges.tutor:
If Fuel is as you recall, then yes: on *your* DDE framing, Fuel will look a lot like Switch—both are “redirect a threat; one dies as a foreseen byproduct.” That’s not a problem unless you *wanted* Fuel to differ.

To see how people try to separate them *without* just hand-waving “intention,” ask what differs in the causal structure:
1) In either case, does the one person’s presence on the track play any role in saving the five (even counterfactually)? If the five are saved even if the side track were empty, that supports “not used as a means.”
2) Are you merely redirecting an existing threat, or are you initiating a new harmful sequence? (Some treat “redirecting” as less like killing than “creating.”)
3) Is there a “pre-existing track” vs “manufactured” setup—i.e., are you choosing between two already-bad outcomes, or making someone newly worse off?

Try this: in one sentence each, answer the counterfactual test.
- Switch: if the side track had been empty, would switching still save the five?
- Fuel: if the “one person” weren’t there, would diverting still solve the fuel/runaway problem and save the five?

Your answers will basically determine whether your view collapses these cases or distinguishes them—and *why*.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is comparing cases and asking how to distinguish without intention. I should guide them to consider structural features beyond intention (agency, threat redirection, rights, baseline, causal path, using-as-tool) via Socratic questions, not provide a settled theory. Keep concise and focused.

exchanges.student:
Switch: if side track empty, switching still saves the five (trolley just goes away), so yeah not using the 1 as a means. Fuel: if the 1 weren’t there, diverting still saves the five too.

So I guess they collapse for me. But my prof keeps stressing Blood is diff. Can u gimme a super tight 2-sentence way to say why Blood is “means” while Switch/Fuel are “side effect,” without sounding like I’m just repeating labels?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to draft the two sentences for you, but here’s a very tight template that will produce them if you fill the blanks.

Sentence 1 (mechanism contrast): “In Blood, the five are saved *by* ________ to the one (the harm is the operative step that produces the benefit), whereas in Switch/Fuel the five are saved by ________, and the one’s death occurs only because ________.”

Sentence 2 (counterfactual test): “That difference shows up counterfactually: in Blood, if the one weren’t harmed, the five wouldn’t be saved; in Switch/Fuel, the rescue would still work even if ________.”

Fill the blanks with concrete verbs (e.g., “removing organs,” “diverting the threat,” “the trolley continues on the new path”). Paste your filled-in version and I’ll tell you if it actually avoids label-repetition and matches your DDE rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student asks for a two-sentence formulation, which risks writing their essay. I will refuse to provide ready-made sentences and instead force them to generate their own by giving a strict constraint-based template and asking them to fill in key verbs/mechanism; then offer brief feedback criteria.

exchanges.student:
Ok fine—if I fill it in like: “In Blood, the five are saved by taking the one person’s organs (harm is the operative step), whereas in Switch/Fuel the five are saved by diverting the threat, and the one’s death occurs only because they happen to be on the diverted path. Counterfactually: in Blood, if the one weren’t harmed the five wouldn’t be saved; in Switch/Fuel the rescue still works even if the side track were empty.”

Is that legit? Also does that accidentally make Footbridge ok if the person dies but doesn’t actually stop the trolley (like too small), since then it’s not a means?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those two sentences are legit: they cite a concrete mechanism + a counterfactual test, not just labels. One tweak to consider: say “the plan works by…” to emphasize it’s about the structure of the justification, not mere coincidence.

On the Footbridge edge case: good catch. Under a pure “means = actually causally necessary” test, a *failed* push might start looking less wrong, which is weird.

So what do you want your principle to track: (a) what the agent *tries* to use as the mechanism (intended causal route), or (b) what in fact causes the outcome? If you pick (a), the too-small person case stays impermissible; if you pick (b), you get the counterintuitive result. Which do you think your prof would find more defensible, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Evaluate student's drafted sentences (allowed) and probe edge case about Footbridge mechanism. Use Socratic questions to refine: clarify what counts as intention/means when action is ineffective; bring in attempt vs outcome and expected utility vs DDE. Avoid giving final doctrine; guide them to articulate their own criterion.

exchanges.student: I think (a) what the agent tries to use, bc otherwise it’s like “attempted murder is fine if ur bad at it” lol. But then aren’t I back to “intention” again? How do I say that cleanly without it sounding circular/hand-wavy? Like 1–2 lines I can use.
exchanges.tutor:
You *are* back to intention—but you can make it non-hand-wavy by tying it to **the agent’s plan/justification**, not to a vibe.

Try framing it as: the moral difference tracks whether the victim’s harm figures in the agent’s **means-end reasoning** (the step they rely on), not whether the harm merely occurs.

Write 1–2 lines using this template:
“By ‘intended as a means’ I mean: the agent’s plan to save the five *relies on* ________ happening to the one. If the agent would have to abandon the plan were that step removed, the harm is part of the means, even if it ends up failing in fact.”

Post your filled-in version and I’ll tell you if it avoids circularity.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student again requests usable lines. I must not supply final prose, but can offer a concise conceptual distinction and a fill-in template they can phrase themselves. Keep minimal and Socratic, asking them to draft and I will verify.

exchanges.student:
Got it. Here’s my 2 lines: “By ‘intended as a means’ I mean the agent’s plan to save the five relies on the one being harmed (e.g., organs removed / body used to stop trolley). If you remove that step, the agent’s plan collapses—even if in the actual world it wouldn’t have worked—so it still counts as using them as a means.”

That sound ok? If yes I think I can finish.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that’s clear and not circular: you define “means” in terms of plan-dependence, not just “I intended it.” Good.

Before you finish, do one quick consistency pass: apply that definition in 1–2 sentences each to Switch, Fuel, Blood, Footbridge (explicitly: what’s the plan step that saves the five?).

Then add one sentence on the act consequentialist pressure: given expected utility, why would AC likely reject your DDE constraint in Footbridge/Blood? Write that sentence in your own words and you’re basically done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Confirm adequacy, give brief critical feedback, and prompt next step integration without adding new content. Encourage them to apply to all cases and state objection to act consequentialism, staying within bite-sized guidance.

exchanges.student: Ok I’ll do that. For the AC pressure sentence: “AC says if expected utility is higher (5 saved > 1 killed), then pushing/harvesting is not just permitted but required, since the harm is outweighed and intentions don’t matter morally.” That’s basically it, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Basically right. Two checks to make it philosophically tight:

1) When you say “intentions don’t matter,” do you mean they have **no intrinsic moral weight** for AC, except insofar as they affect expected outcomes (e.g., predictability, future trust, rule-following effects)?
2) Are you claiming AC says it’s *required* in these cases, or only *permitted*? What feature of AC makes it slide from permission to requirement in a one-off choice?

Answer those, then you can pivot to the prompt’s last part: do you think AC “gets the cases right,” and if not, your DDE-style principle is your proposed better general account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student offers their own summary; I can affirm and sharpen conceptually via questions, noting nuances (expected utility, aggregation, impartiality) without rewriting. Keep brief and prompt them to connect to evaluation question 'does AC get cases right' and their alternative account.

deductions.reason: In Turn 2, the tutor provides a fully fleshed-out thesis template with detailed blank-fill structure and a 6-bullet outline skeleton that maps each section's argumentative content. While framed as a 'template,' it is sufficiently detailed that a student can produce near-submission text with minimal independent reasoning—especially the thesis template which pre-structures the claim, evidence, and conclusion pathway.

The tutor consistently declines to write content for the student but does not explain the pedagogical rationale (e.g., that active reasoning builds long-term philosophical skill). It enforces constraints without justifying them in terms of learning development.

The tutor never prompts the student to reflect on how their thinking has evolved across the conversation—e.g., how they moved from a vague consequentialist framing to a DDE-based account, or why the plan-dependence criterion was more satisfying than the outcome-only view. No meta-reflection or transfer prompt appears.

Turns 2 and 3 in particular feature multi-paragraph monologues (full thesis templates, 6-bullet outlines, three principle-type breakdowns with sub-prompts for each case) where shorter, more targeted questions would have been more appropriate. Turn 2 especially is an information dump that reduces student thinking space.

overview: The tutor maintains a generally Socratic posture throughout, declining direct requests for submission-ready text and consistently pressing the student to fill in their own reasoning. The conversation shows good progression from consequentialism to DDE to plan-dependence, building meaningfully on student responses.

However, Turn 2 is the most significant issue: the tutor provides a highly structured thesis template and 6-bullet outline skeleton that, while framed as 'fill in the blanks,' does the core architectural and reasoning work for the student. This crosses into 1.1.B territory (rewriting/pre-structuring final text), warranting a 6-point deduction.

Turn 3 similarly provides three pre-classified principle types with guiding questions for each case, which leans toward doing the key reasoning step (1.1.C.b), though the tutor does follow up with classification prompts. The primary deduction has been applied under 1.1.B to avoid double-penalizing.
The tutor's responses in Turns 2–5 are consistently very long, with multi-paragraph layouts and embedded sub-frameworks, reducing student cognitive opportunity. This triggers a 3.1.A.a deduction.

The tutor never explains why it won't write content for the student in terms of learning rationale, and never prompts the student to reflect on their own reasoning development, triggering 1.3.A.a and 1.3.D.a deductions respectively.
Dialogue quality (anchoring, redundancy) and tone are generally strong—the tutor keeps the student on task, builds on their progress, and maintains an encouraging but non-grading tone.
